sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 57-year-old male, presents with a complaint of shortness of breath for approximately two weeks.
He reports waking up with shortness of breath, which persists throughout the day, even after resting.
He mentions coughing up blood, approximately three to four cups per day, which has been ongoing for three weeks.
He denies any fever, bruising, or bleeding from other areas.
He has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
His diabetes has been running high, with blood sugar levels spiking over 200 after meals and ranging from 150 to 200 in the morning.
His blood pressure has been low at times, ranging from the lower 90s.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
Review of systems:
The patient reports coughing up blood, shortness of breath, and high blood sugar levels.
Current medications:
The patient is taking metformin 500 mg twice daily for diabetes and norvasc for high blood pressure.
Objective:
Vital signs:
The patient's oxygen saturation is 94% on pulse oximetry, and his pulse is regular.
Physical exam:
The patient has no jugular venous distension, no carotid bruits, and no swelling in the neck.
The lung exam reveals bilateral rales and rhonchi, with diminished breath sounds on the right side.
The heart exam shows a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, unchanged from previous exams.
The belly exam is soft, and the extremity exam reveals 1+ non-pitting edema on both lower extremities, with no calf tenderness or Homan's sign.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has a possible pulmonary infection or other lung condition, as evidenced by coughing up blood and shortness of breath, which is causing his primary complaint.
The patient also has poorly controlled diabetes, with high blood sugar levels.
Additionally, the patient has high blood pressure.
Problem list:
Pulmonary infection or other lung condition; Poorly controlled diabetes; High blood pressure.
Plan:
I prescribed metformin 750 mg twice daily to increase the dose for diabetes management.
I will order a hemoglobin A1c to assess the patient's diabetes control.
I will have the patient return in three weeks to discuss his diabetes treatment plan.
I referred the patient to the emergency department for further evaluation and treatment of the shortness of breath and coughing up blood.
The patient will undergo a chest X-ray, CT scan of the chest, and blood work to rule out a blood clot or other conditions.
I instructed the patient to hold taking norvasc until his blood pressure is under better control.
Follow-up:
The patient will return to the emergency department for further evaluation and treatment.
The patient will return in three weeks to discuss his diabetes treatment plan.
